perturbation.original_text: 16. Term . The foregoing commitments in this Agreement will survive any termination of discussions between the parties, and will continue for a period of two (2) years following the date of this Agreement, except to the extent explicitly terminated in writing by both ZipRealty and Company.
perturbation.changed_text: 16. Term . The foregoing commitments in this Agreement will survive any termination of discussions between the parties, and will continue indefinitely following the date of this Agreement, except to the extent explicitly terminated in writing by both ZipRealty and Company.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the term to 'indefinitely' could be problematic as California law favors contracts with definite terms, especially in the context of trade secrets. An indefinite term may be challenged as unreasonable restraint of trade.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' An indefinite term for confidentiality, while not a direct restraint on trade, can be construed as an unreasonable restraint, particularly if it hinders future employment or business opportunities, potentially conflicting with this statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the term of the agreement from two years to 'indefinitely.' California Business and Professions Code Section 16600(a), as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, indicates that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' While a confidentiality agreement isn't a direct restraint of trade, an indefinite term can be interpreted as an unreasonable restraint, hindering future employment or business opportunities, thus conflicting with the statute. The change from a definite two-year term to 'indefinitely' introduces this potential conflict, making the contract potentially unenforceable under California law.

perturbation.original_text: 4. Disclosure Required by Law . In the event that the receiving party or any of its Representatives is requested or required by legal process to disclose any of the Confidential Information of the disclosing party, the receiving party will, if legally permissible, give prompt written notice to the disclosing party so that the disclosing party may seek, at its expense, a protective order or other appropriate relief.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Disclosure Required by Law . In the event that the receiving party or any of its Representatives is requested or required by legal process to disclose any of the Confidential Information of the disclosing party, the receiving party will only notify the disclosing party after disclosing the information.
perturbation.explanation: California law requires notification prior to disclosure if legally permissible. This modification makes notification occur after disclosure, potentially violating legal and ethical duties.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 2016.070
perturbation.law_explanation: Although 2016.070 is focused on protective orders for discovery, it highlights the general duty to protect confidential information and seek protective measures, like a protective order, when disclosure is compelled. Waiting until *after* disclosure severely limits the disclosing party's ability to seek such protection, which could be interpreted as a failure to take reasonable steps to maintain confidentiality, contrary to common practices and legal expectations in California.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 2016.070 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 4. CIVIL DISCOVERY ACT [2016.010 - 2036.050] ( Title 4 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) CHAPTER 1. General Provisions [2016.010 - 2016.090] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) 2016.070. This title applies to discovery in aid of enforcement of a money judgment only to the extent provided in Article 1 (commencing with Section 708.010) of Chapter 6 of Title 9 of Part 2. (Added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. Effective January 1, 2005. Operative July 1, 2005, by Sec. 64 of Ch. 182.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original intent of protecting confidential information by delaying notification until after disclosure. The original text mandates prompt notification to allow the disclosing party to seek a protective order, as is common legal practice. By waiting until after disclosure, the opportunity to seek protection is significantly diminished, violating common practices and legal expectations. Although the provided snippet focuses on discovery, the Law Explanation highlights the importance of protecting confidential information, which the changed text undermines.

perturbation.original_text: 13. Governing Law and Jurisdiction . This Agreement will be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the internal laws of the State of California without regard to its conflict-of-laws principles. The federal and state courts within the State of California will have exclusive jurisdiction to adjudicate any dispute arising out of this Agreement. Each party irrevocably consents to the personal jurisdiction of such courts and expressly waives any objection to such jurisdiction based on inconvenient forum or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. Governing Law and Jurisdiction . This Agreement will be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the internal laws of the State of Delaware without regard to its conflict-of-laws principles. The federal and state courts within the State of Delaware will have exclusive jurisdiction to adjudicate any dispute arising out of this Agreement. Each party irrevocably consents to the personal jurisdiction of such courts and expressly waives any objection to such jurisdiction based on inconvenient forum or otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: Although both parties are Delaware corporations, operating primarily within California and having significant contacts there can make a choice of Delaware law unenforceable under California choice-of-law rules if it violates a fundamental policy of California law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 410.30
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure 410.30 allows California courts to decline to exercise jurisdiction when it is a seriously inconvenient forum. Furthermore, California courts may refuse to enforce a choice-of-law provision if it violates a fundamental public policy of California. If the application of Delaware law would substantially diminish the protections afforded by California's laws related to trade secrets or employee solicitation, a California court might refuse to enforce the Delaware choice-of-law provision. This is especially true because one of the entities is located in California.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 410.30 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 2. Forum [410.30 - 410.42] ( Heading of Article 2 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 4. ) 410.30. (a) When a court upon motion of a party or its own motion finds that in the interest of substantial justice an action should be heard in a forum outside this state, the court shall stay or dismiss the action in whole or in part on any conditions that may be just. (b) The provisions of Section 418.10 do not apply to a motion to stay or dismiss the action by a defendant who has made a general appearance. (Amended by Stats. 1972, Ch. 601. Note: This version was suspended from Sept. 22, 1986, until Jan. 1, 1992, during operation of the temporary amendment by Stats. 1986, Ch. 968.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the governing law and jurisdiction from California to Delaware. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state that choosing Delaware law may be unenforceable if it violates a fundamental policy of California law, particularly concerning trade secrets or employee solicitation. This is supported by the scraped snippet from California Code of Civil Procedure 410.30, which allows California courts to decline jurisdiction if it is a seriously inconvenient forum. While 410.30 speaks to forum selection and not choice of law directly, the Law Explanation connects it to the broader principle that California courts may refuse to enforce a choice-of-law provision if it violates a fundamental public policy of California. Therefore, changing the governing law to Delaware creates a potential contradiction because it could lead to the application of laws that undermine California's public policy, especially if one of the entities is located in California, potentially resulting in a violation of California law.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Return of Materials . Any materials or documents that have been furnished by or on behalf of the disclosing party to the receiving party or its Representatives will, promptly upon the disclosing party’s request, at the receiving party’s option, either (i) be returned to the disclosing party, and all copies of such documentation will be destroyed, or (ii) be destroyed (along with all copies of such documentation), such destruction to be certified in writing to the disclosing party.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Return of Materials . Any materials or documents that have been furnished by or on behalf of the disclosing party to the receiving party or its Representatives will, within 180 days of the disclosing party’s request, at the receiving party’s option, either (i) be returned to the disclosing party, and all copies of such documentation will be destroyed, or (ii) be destroyed (along with all copies of such documentation), such destruction to be certified in writing to the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the return or destruction of confidential materials by 180 days after the request could be seen as a failure to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets under California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: While CUTSA doesn't specify a precise timeframe for returning materials, it emphasizes the protection of trade secrets from misappropriation. A 180-day delay in returning or destroying confidential information after a request could be viewed as commercially unreasonable and could increase the risk of misappropriation, especially if the information is highly sensitive or rapidly changing. This delay could be interpreted as a failure to take reasonable measures to protect the trade secret, potentially undermining the disclosing party's rights under CUTSA should misappropriation occur during that period.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a 180-day delay in the return or destruction of confidential materials, which contradicts the spirit of CUTSA. While the provided snippet only cites the title of the act (Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS...TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11]), the Law Explanation argues that this delay could be seen as a failure to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, potentially undermining the disclosing party's rights. Given the 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' definition, which identifies contradictions arising when time-sensitive obligations do not align with legal requirements, and the Perturbation Explanation's assertion that this delay could violate California law, a contradiction exists. The modified text introduces a timeframe that could increase the risk of misappropriation, conflicting with the act's goal of protecting trade secrets.
